''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim i]]s an official holiday in modern Egypt. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.sa11ara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival now is known as [[Sham Ennessim]], and it has been nationally celebrated by all the Egyptians since ancient times, as it is considered a national festival in Modern Egypt as it was related to the agricultural background of the ancient Egyptians, originating from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim i]]s an official holiday in modern Egypt. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.sa11ara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival now is known as [[Sham Ennessim]], and it has been nationally celebrated by all the Egyptians since ancient times, as it is considered a national festival in Modern Egypt as it was related to the agricultural background of the ancient Egyptians, originating from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim i]]s an official holiday in modern Egypt. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.sa11ara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: special import from Wikipedia just to avoid the bot issue so that I can still have these tagges as originating from wikipedia placing the revisions a few days into the future
revision.text:
{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]
''Prt'' |title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Harvest
''Šmw'' |years=days: 125 or 126 days |after=[[Egyptian intercalary month|Days over the Year]]
''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

revision.text: The Season of the Harvest refers to the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use flint knives and sickles to cut off grain heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the granaries and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then seperate the grain from the chaff.

The '''Season of the Harvest''' refers to the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [[granary|granaries]] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then seperate the grain from the [[chaff]].

The '''Season of the Harvest''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [[granary|granaries]] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then seperate the grain from the [[chaff]].

During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [[granary|granaries]] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then seperate the grain from the [[chaff]].

The '''Season of the Harvest''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows the [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [[granary|granaries]] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then separate the grain from the [[chaff]].

The '''Season of the Harvest''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows the [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[Cereal|grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [[granary|granaries]] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then separate the grain from the [[chaff]].

The '''Season of the Harvest''' is the ancient [[History of Egypt|Egyptian]] [[Fall]] and follows the [[Season of the Going Out]]. During this period the Egyptians would use [[flint]] knives and [[sickle]]s to cut off [[Cereal|grain]] heads and collect the grain into sacks which were carried to the [http://wiktionary.org/w/wiki.phtml?title=Granary granaries] and beaten with a [[flail]]. [[Wind winnowing]] would then separate the grain from the [[chaff]].

The '''Season of the Harvest''' [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''shmw'', <hiero>S-mw-hrw</hiero>), is the third and last season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]], following the [[Season of the Emergence]]. It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time.
The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of [[March]] to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the ancient Egyptians occured on [[July]] 17-19 (Julian). According to Richard A. Parker, to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year.
Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days.
[[category:Ancient Egypt]]

It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time.
The ancient Egyptians used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of [[March]] to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the ancient Egyptians occured on [[July]] 17-19 (Julian). According to Richard A. Parker, to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year.
Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days.

According to Richard A. Parker, to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. [[Category:Ancient Egyptian society]]

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – shmw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' (in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''shmw''), is the third and last season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]], following the [[Season of the Emergence]]. It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to Richard A. Parker, to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year. {{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – shmw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' (in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] ''shmw'' – occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu]), is the third and last season in the ancient [[Egyptian calendar]], following the [[Season of the Emergence]]. It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. The word Shemu translates literally as "low-water" and corresponded roughly from mid-March to mid-July when the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. ==References==

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' (the [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] term or '''Shemu''' is [[Manuel_de_Codage|transliterated]] as Smw – and occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu]), The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water" and corresponded roughly from mid-March to mid-July when the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. It is also known as the summer or 'dry' season[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. ==References==

The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water" and corresponded roughly from mid-March to mid-July when the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. It is also known as the summer or 'dry' season[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an intercalendary month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 3 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalendary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. ==References==

Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an [[intercalary]] month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 4 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. ==References==

Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' It is also known as the summer or 'dry' season[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an [[intercalary]] month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 4 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar; it consists of the four 30-day months of Pachons, Payni, Epiphi and Mesore, and a five-day period that was considered to be an intercalary period which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. [[File:shemu.jpg]] Or simply a mystical animal crossed between a sheep and an emu. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References==

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The Egyptian term '''Season of the Harvest''' (the [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] or '''Shemu''' is [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw – and occasionally written as '''Shomu'''). The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water" and corresponded roughly from mid-March to mid-July when the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian peasants would harvest the ir crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. It is also known as the summer or 'dry' season[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an [[intercalary]] month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 4 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. [[File:shemu.jpg]] Or simply a mystical animal crossed between a sheep and an emu. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References==
[[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[de:Szemu]] [[fr:Chémou]] [[hu:Semu]] [[pl:Szemu]]

This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the civil year to a total of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References==

Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' ''Akhet''}} {{end box}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[de:Szemu]] [[fr:Chémou]] [[hu:Semu]] [[pl:Szemu]]

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The Egyptian term '''Season of the Harvest''' (the [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] or '''Shemu''' is [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw – and occasionally written as '''Shomu'''). The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water" and corresponded roughly from mid-March to mid-July when the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was the third and final season of the Ancient Egyptian calendar (the other two were ''[[akhet]]'' and ''[[Peret (season)|peret]]).'' It received its name because this was the period when the Egyptian Farmers would harvest their crops -- in clear variance to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who had just begun to plant their crops at this time. It is also known as the summer or 'dry' season[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from the middle of March to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]], which during the time of the Egyptians occurred on July 17-19 (Julian). According to [[Richard Anthony Parker|Richard A. Parker]], to keep their lunar calendar in harmony with the rising of this star, an [[intercalary]] month (named Thoth, after the god, which meant the year often had two months with the same name) would be added to the end of the season, usually every 4 years, but sometimes every second year. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the season of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] and is preceded by [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References==

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} '''Season of the Harvest''' (known in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''' - [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw) is the third and final season of the [[Egyptian calendar]]. The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water", and falls roughly between early May and early September. During this season, the crops of the grain harvest are collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' is preceded by the [[Season of Emergence]] ('''Proyet'''), and is followed by the [[Season of Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.[http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu Britannica: shomu] The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early May to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]] around the beginning of September. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the season of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Coptic calendar]] ==References==

Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the season of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0871692147, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{start box}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

Season of the Harvest
'''''Shemu'''''| years=days: 125 or 126 days| after=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Akhet''}} {{end box}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[de:Schemu]] [[fr:Chémou]] [[lt:Šemu]] [[pl:Szemu]] [[ru:Шему]]

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:,:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} '''Season of the Harvest''' (known in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''' - [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw) is the third and final season of the [[Egyptian calendar]]. The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water", and falls roughly between early May and early September. During this season, the crops of the grain harvest are collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] ('''Proyet'''), and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=shomu |publisher=Britannica |date= |accessdate=2012-03-14}} The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early May to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]] around the beginning of September. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the season of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

''Akhet''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}} [[de:Schemu]] [[fr:Chémou]] [[lt:Šemu]] [[pl:Szemu]] [[ru:Шему]]

The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water", and falls roughly between early May and early September. During this season, the crops of the grain harvest are collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] ('''Proyet'''), and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=shomu |publisher=Britannica |date= |accessdate=2012-03-14}} The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early May to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]] around the beginning of September. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consists of four 30-day months plus one little month of 5 or 6 days. This little month was considered to be an intercalary period, which brought the season of 365 days. These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni]] * Month 11 = [[Epip]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori]] * Month 13 = [[Pi Kogi Enavot]] (the little month) The Season of Harvest is preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and is followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

Season of the Harvest
'''''Shemu'''''| years=days: 125 or 126 days| after=[[Season of the Inundation]]
''Akhet''}} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian calendar]] {{Ancient-Egypt-stub}}

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' (known in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''' - [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw) was the third and final season of the [[Egyptian calendar]]. The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water", and fell roughly between early May and early September. During this season, the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] ('''Peret'''), and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=shomu |publisher=Britannica |date= |accessdate=2012-03-14}} The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early May to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]] around the beginning of September. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consisted of four 30-day months after which followed a period of 5 [[epagomenal day]]s, which brought the year to 365 days.Allen, James P. (2000). Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs. Cambridge University press. pp. 103-106 These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons|Hnsw]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni|Hnt-htj]] * Month 11 = [[Epip|Ipt-hmt]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori|Wep-renpet]] The Season of Harvest was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consisted of four 30-day months after which followed a period of 5 [[epagomenal day]]s, which brought the year to 365 days.Allen, James P. (2000). Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs. Cambridge University press. pp. 103-106 These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons|Hnsw]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni|Hnt-htj]] * Month 11 = [[Epip|Ipt-hmt]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori|Wep-renpet]] The Season of Harvest was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Sham el-Nessim|Sham Ennesim]] *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

{{Hiero|The Season of the
Harvest – Smw|N37:mw-hrw-.- or -.-N37:N36-t:N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' (known in [[Egyptian language|Egyptian]] as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''' - [[Manuel de Codage|transliterated]] as Smw) was the third and final season of the [[Egyptian calendar]]. The word Shemu literally translates as "low-water", and fell roughly between early May and early September. During this season, the crops of the grain harvest were collected.David P. Silverman, Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] ('''Peret'''), and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=shomu |publisher=Britannica |date= |accessdate=2012-03-14}} The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. The months of the lunar calendar are roughly equivalent to the period from early May to the heliacal rising of [[Sirius]] around the beginning of September. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consisted of four 365-day months after which followed a period of 5 [[epagomenal day]]s, which brought the year to 365 days.Allen, James P. (2000). Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs. Cambridge University press. pp. 103-106 These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons|Hnsw]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni|Hnt-htj]] * Month 11 = [[Epip|Ipt-hmt]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori|Wep-renpet]] The Season of Harvest was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Sham el-Nessim|Sham Ennesim]] *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 '''Shemu''' was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] ('''Peret'''), and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]] ('''Akhet'''). During '''Shemu''', the Egyptian farmers would harvest their crops - in clear contrast to the practice of their contemporaries in the ancient world, who would be just beginning to plant their crops at this time of the year. '''Shemu''' is also known as the '''Summer Season''' or the '''Dry Season'''[http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html Leiden University] and is occasionally written as '''Shomu'''.{{cite web|url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=shomu |publisher=Britannica |date= |accessdate=2012-03-14}} The ancient [[Egyptians]] used this name in both their lunar and their civil calendars. Since the civil calendar moved through the seasons over time, losing about one day every four years, this season does not continuously match any part of the modern calendar. ==Months== The Season of Harvest consisted of four 30-day months after which followed a period of 5 [[epagomenal day]]s, which brought the year to 365 days.Allen, James P. Cambridge University press. pp. 103-106 These months can be either referred to by number (months 9 through 13) or by names as follows: * Month 9 = [[Pashons|Hnsw]] * Month 10 = [[Paoni|Hnt-htj]] * Month 11 = [[Epip|Ipt-hmt]] * Month 12 = [[Mesori|Birth of the Sun]] (''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'') The Season of Harvest was preceded by the [[Season of the Emergence]] and was followed by the [[Season of the Inundation]].Marshall Clagett, ''Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book'', Diane 1989, ISBN 0-87169-214-7, p.5 ==See also== *[[Sham el-Nessim|Sham Ennesim]] *[[Coptic calendar]] *[[Egyptian calendar]] *[[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==References== {{Reflist}} {{S-start}} {{succession box| before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A-N5, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{fact}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' ({{lang-egy|[[transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Šmw]]}}) was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt'').{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book |date=1989 |isbn=0-87169-214-7 |p=5 }}. {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided between four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}

''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}

''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''

Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided between four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A-N5, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{fact}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt'').{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book |date=1989 |isbn=0-87169-214-7 |p=5 }}. {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "'''Low{{nbsp}}Water'''" ({{lang-egy|[[transliteration of ancient Egyptian|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided between four months. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.let.leidenuniv.nl/saqqara/Egyptology/Glossary/Inundation.html |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided between four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=American Philosophical Society |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{fact|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decan]]s. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=American Philosophical Society |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{fact|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}

Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |}

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=American Philosophical Society |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{fact|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=Leiden University }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103-106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. (just so you know it is October) In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].
