revision.text:
{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].
{| class="wikitable" border="1" cellpadding="5" align="center" | style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |}

{{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as ''''Summer'''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&printsec=frontcover |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]}}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pp=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].
{| class="wikitable" align="center" | style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |}

Season of the Harvest

''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. In the modern [[Coptic calendar]] it falls between [[Tobi 11]] and [[Paoni 11]].{{Cite web |title=Image 8 of The Divine Liturgies of Saints Basil, Gregory, and Cyril |url=https://www.loc.gov/resource/ihas.200155823.0/?sp=8 |access-date=2022-11-01 |website=Library of Congress, Washington, D.C. 20540 USA |pages=171-175}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]].
{| class="wikitable" align="center" | style="margin: 1em auto 1em auto" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{harvp|Tetley|2014|p=[http://www.egyptchronology.com/uploads/2/6/9/4/26943741/ch_3_investigating_ancient_egyptian_calendars.pdf 39]}}.{{sfnp|Winlock|1940|p=453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{lang-egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''

{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''

It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival now is known as [[Sham Ennessim]], and it has been nationally celebrated by all the Egyptians since ancient times, as it is considered a national festival in Modern Egypt as it was related to the agricultural background of the ancient Egyptians, originating from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim i]]s an official holiday in modern Egypt. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival now is known as [[Sham Ennessim]], and it has been nationally celebrated by all the Egyptians since ancient times, as it is considered a national festival in Modern Egypt as it was related to the agricultural background of the ancient Egyptians, originating from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is]] an official holiday in modern Egypt. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! revision.comment: Edited the Shemu > Sham Ennesim claim - if you have strong evidence to show that there was a šm.w festival and that it was preserved into Coptic times and then became the modern holiday please cite related sources.

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. [[Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is]] an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! {{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}

Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]
''Prt'' |title=''[[Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Harvest
''Šmw'' |years=days: 125 or 126 days |after=[[Egyptian intercalary month|Days over the Year]]
''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Season of the Harvest]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[:en:David P. Silverman|David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}
New Year's
Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]
''Prt'' |title=''[[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian Seasons]]''
Season of the Harvest
''Šmw'' |years=days: 125 or 126 days |after=[[:en:Egyptian intercalary month|Days over the Year]]
''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Season of the Harvest]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[:en:David P. Silverman|David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
''{{lang|egy|Mswt Rꜥ}}'' || {{nowrap|Fourth Month of Low Water}}

Birth of the [[Ra (god)|Sun]] || [[Mesori]] |- |} ==See also== * [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] & [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]s * [[Ancient Egyptian units of measurement#Time|Egyptian units of time]] * [[Sham Ennessim]] * [[Temple of Kom Ombo]] ==Notes== {{Noteslist}} ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} {{S-start}} {{succession box |before=[[Season of the Emergence]]

''Ḥryw Rnpt'' }} {{S-end}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Season Of The Harvest}} [[Category:Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Season of the Harvest]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|3rd and final season of the lunar and civil Egyptian calendars}} {{redirect|Low Water}} {{Hiero|{{nowrap|Season of Low Water{{citation |first=Marshall |last=Clagett |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC |title=Ancient Egyptian Science: A Source Book, ''Vol. II:'' Calendars, Clocks, and Astronomy |location=Philadelphia |publisher=[[American Philosophical Society]] |series=''Memoirs of the APS'', No. 214 |date=1995 |page=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA5 5]|isbn=9780871692146 }}.{{efn|Alternative representations of the Season of Low Water include N37-N5, N37:N35-N35:N35, N37-N35A, N37-Z5-N5, and N37-Z5-Z5{{citation |last=Vygus |first=Mark |date=2015 |title=Middle Egyptian Dictionary |url=http://www.pyramidtextsonline.com/documents/VygusDictionaryApril2015.pdf }}. and N37:N36-t:N5.{{citation needed|date=February 2017}}}}}}
''Šmw''|N37-N35A-N5|align=right|era=default}} The '''Season of the Harvest''' or '''Low Water''' was the third and final season of the lunar and civil [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]]s. It fell after the [[Season of the Emergence]] (''{{lang|egy|Prt}}'') and before the spiritually dangerous [[intercalary month (Egypt)|intercalary month]] (''{{lang|egy|Ḥryw Rnpt}}''), after which the New Year's festivities began the [[Season of the Inundation]] (''Ꜣḫt''). In the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] and [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] calendars this season begins at the start of the month of [[Pashons]] (about 9 May), continues through the months of [[Paoni]] and [[:en:Epip|Epip]], before concluding at the end of [[Mesori]] (about 5 September).{{sfnp|Clagett|1995|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=xKKPUpDOTKAC&pg=PA4 14–15]}}{{citation |last=Tetley |first=M. Christine |date=2014 |title=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |volume=1 |location=Whangarei, New Zealand |publisher=Barry W. Tetley |isbn=978-0-473-29338-3 |url=https://www.ancientportsantiques.com/wp-content/uploads/Documents/PLACES/Egypt-Libya/EgyptianChronology1-Tetley2014.pdf |access-date=26 September 2023 |page=39}}{{citation |last=Winlock |first=Herbert Eustis |author-link=Herbert Eustis Winlock |date=1940 |contribution-url=https://archive.org/stream/H.e.WinlockTheOriginOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar1840/Winlock_originOfTheAncientEgyptianCalendar_procAmerPhilosophSoc_1940_sep_v83_n3_447-464#page/n0/mode/2up |contribution=The Origin of the Ancient Egyptian Calendar |pages=447–464 |title=Proceedings of the American Philosophical Society, ''No. 83'' |location=New York |publisher=Metropolitan Museum of Art }}{{rp|453}} {{anchor|Etymology|Name}} The festival known as [[Sham Ennessim]], is often claimed to have originated from [[Season of the Harvest|Shemu]]. Sham Ennessim|Sham Ennessim is an official holiday in modern Egypt. Note, however, that šm.w relates to the season in Egyptian, not a specific festival. ==Names== The Season of the Harvest was known to the Egyptians themselves as "Low{{nbsp}}Water" ({{langx|egy|[[wikt:šmw|Šmw]]}}), variously transliterated as '''Shemu''' or '''Shomu''',{{citation |contribution-url=http://www.britannica.com/eb/topic-541508/shomu |title=Encyclopaedia Britannica |contribution=Shomu |accessdate=14 March 2012 }}. in reference to the state of the [[Nile]] before the beginning of [[:en:Flooding of the Nile|its annual flood]]. It is also referred to as '''Summer''' or the '''Dry Season'''.{{citation |contribution=Inundation |contribution-url=http://www.saqqara.nl/context/glossary/inundation |title=Glossary |publisher=[[Leiden University]] }}. ==Lunar calendar== In the lunar calendar, the intercalary month was added as needed to maintain the [[heliacal rising]] of [[Sirius (star)|Sirius]] in the [[Mesori|fourth month]] of this season. This meant that the Season of the Harvest usually lasted from May to September. Because the precise timing of the flood varied, the months of "Low Water" no longer precisely reflected the state of the river but the season was usually the time for the collection of Egypt's grain harvest.[[:en:David P. Silverman|David P. Silverman]], Ancient Egypt, Duncan Baird Publishers, London 1997. p.93 ==Civil calendar== In the civil calendar, the lack of leap years into the [[Ptolemaic Egypt|Ptolemaic]] and [[Roman Egypt|Roman periods]] meant the season lost about one day every four years and was not stable relative to the [[solar year]] or [[Gregorian calendar]]. ==Months== The Season of the Harvest was divided into four months. In the lunar calendar, each began on a dawn when the waning crescent moon was no longer visible. In the civil calendar, each consisted of exactly 30 days{{citation |last=Allen |first=James P. |date=2000 |title=Middle Egyptian: An Introduction to the Language and Culture of Hieroglyphs |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |location=[[Cambridge, England|Cambridge]] |pages=103–106 }}. divided into three 10-day weeks known as [[:en:Decans|Decans]]. In ancient Egypt, these months were usually recorded by their number within the season: I, II, III, and IV ''Šmw''. They were also known by the names of their principal festivals, which came to be increasingly used after the [[Persian Egypt|Persian occupation]]. These then became the basis for the names of the months of the [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic calendar]]. {| class="wikitable" style="margin:1em auto;" |- ! colspan=2 | [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian]] ! rowspan=2 | [[:en:Coptic calendar|Coptic]] |- ! [[Transliteration of Ancient Egyptian|Transliteration]] ! Meaning |- | I ''Šmw''
''Hnsw'' || {{nowrap|First Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Pashons]] |- | II ''Šmw''
''Hnt-Hty'' || {{nowrap|Second Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[Paoni]] |- | III ''Šmw''
''Ipt-Hmt'' || {{nowrap|Third Month of Low Water}}
{{nbsp}} || [[:en:Epip|Epip]] |- | IV ''Šmw''
''{{lang|egy|Wp Rnpt}}''
